title: Touch, Tell, Sell, Care.
body:
For the digital marketing plan we use the touch, tell, sell, care model. This theory will help you structure your marketing plan from gathered information into action.

The different phases explained:


  • The Touch phase focuses on awareness of the brand and products. Think of a radio, television or display advertising campaign. For example a flashy inspiring Zalando banner showing the newest Fall/Winter collection of a certain brand.

  • The Tell phase creates interest and desire. For example by means of landing pages on the website telling more about the special features of the product. Examples of marketing means of this phase are: email campaigns, social media, newsletters and de product detail page.

  • The Sell phase wishes to convert traffic into customers. Usually in the webshop or market place. Most effective marketing in this phase is Sponsored Products.

  • The ultimate phase is Care and focuses on a happy match between the customer and his ordered goods or services. Ultimately, to retain the customer and create a customer lifetime value. Social media marketing, blogs, newsletters, special discounts and reviews are used marketing means.


Traditional Touch Tell Sell Care flow, with marketing means by phase:

title: MerchantSpring - Accountmanagement
body:
MerchantSpring - Accountmanagement

In this academy, we will share the process of performing account management for all clients. Depending on the channel, a variety of information is available. This is due to API limitations per channel. 

Amazon Accountmanagement

When checking the account performance for Amazon, check the following twice per week.

Account Health:

  • This can be found via My Channels > Operations > Account Health
  • The Account Health overview has a set date range, this can't be adjusted. If any of the metrics are underperforming VS targets, please reach out to your consultant so they can discuss the next steps with the client.

Listing Management

  • Go to Suppressed listings and solve any new products added to the list. This has to be done directly in Seller Central.

Once per week:

  • Update the replenishment overview (see Replenishment Academy)
  • Go to FBA Inventory and Check 'unfulfillable inventory', create removal order if needed

The following metrics are NOT available in MerchantSpring:

  • Open customer questions
  • Compliance issues or other account notifications.

Zalando Accountmanagement

For now, it is not possible to perform Zalando account management via MerchantSpring due to API limitations. Follow the instructions below to perform account management for the Zalando marketplaces outside of MerchantSpring.

Twice per week, check the following metrics:

  • Monitor the CXM model for the client, if there are changes inform the consultant (Non-ZFS clients only)
  • Check if the advertisements are still running accordingly, meaning:
    • Do the campaigns still have a sufficient budget?
    • Do any countries have relatively high ACOS?

Once per week:

  • Update the replenishment overview (see Replenishment Academy)

Bol Accountmanagement

The account performance of Bol should be checked every Thursday:

  • This can be found via My Channels > Operations > Account Health
  • The Account Health overview has a set date range, this can't be adjusted. If any of the metrics are underperforming VS targets, please reach out to your consultant so they can discuss the next steps with the client.

The following metrics are NOT available in MerchantSpring:

  • Open customer questions
  • Other account notifications.

How to report on Account Health to your client

The operational performance of the marketplace is discussed in your (bi-)weekly meeting with the client. Here you should always notify the clients regarding the status of their accounts. To make sure the client is up-to-date, always send an email to the client (this should also be done after your meeting with the notes) where you mention the following:

  • Explain the metric that is underperforming
  • What should the client do to increase this performance?
  • Explain the potential consequences if the metric keeps underperforming

title: Manual campaign
body:
A manual targeting campaign is much more fragile than an automatic targeting campaign. A seller handpicks keywords corresponding with the products they sell. Your ad will only come up if the customer is using a search term that matches the keywords the seller put in, depending on the match type.

 

To make this campaign a success, every keyword needs to be carefully selected. This will make your campaign more precise. Only showing up for the specific keywords that are relevant to the product that is being targeted. Yet, this is time-consuming and you are at risk of paying for keywords that are not relevant to your product and possibly lead to poor conversion. For this reason, it is always wise to run an automatic campaign next to your manual campaign, so you can see what keywords are automatically being found and used by Amazon, and which ones work. It is labour intensive because every keyword is added manually. 


For a manual campaign, you can determine what kind of match type you want for a keyword:

Broad match:
Your ad will be triggered when a shopper searches for your keywords in any order, along with other close variants. While broad match keywords do not guarantee the best returns, they are a great way to gather data. Start with a broad match to find new keywords and improve ad visibility.

Phrase match: Your ad will be displayed if a buyer searches for the keywords in the same order along with either suffixes or prefixes.

Exact match: The ad will only be displayed when a buyer searches for the exact keywords. The search term cannot include any other words or phrases. This is the most refined targeting option. High-converting keywords with exact matches generally have lower ACoS. This comes at the cost of visibility that you would have received if you were using a broad match or phrase match targeting.

When getting started, broad is the recommended option.


In general, the strategy for manual campaigns should be as follows:

  • Run an automatic campaign with a relatively low budget to discover well-converting keywords.
  • Use these keywords in a manual targeting campaign that runs simultaneously with the automatic targeting campaign.
  • Analyse both campaigns and eliminate negative keywords to prevent unnecessary ad spending. 


When running an automatic campaign and a manual campaign at the same time, you will make sure that you stay on top of search trends and, as mentioned before, find the most effective keywords.


How to create a manual campaign:

  1. Log in on ASC (for the client you are working for) → Click: ‘advertising’ → ‘campaign manager’.

  2. Perform a search term analysis, Check the client scan for extra information about previous search term analysis for this client and extra information about their products.
        -   Automatic campaigns: pay attention to the number of clicks relative to sales and ACOS. (module 4.4)
        -   Helium 10: Pay attention to search volume and IQ score. (module 4.2)

    Add all the keywords (or product ASINs) you are using for this manual campaign to the negative keywords in the automatic campaign! By doing this, you prevent these campaigns from driving up the price by bidding against one another.

  3. Update the client scan with important (or negative) search terms you found. 

  4. Make sure the campaign manager is set to the right country (you can find/change this in the top right corner of the screen)
  5. Click: ‘create portfolio’ and enter the title Manual-basics → Click: ‘create a portfolio'

  6. Click: ‘create campaign’ and choose ‘sponsored products’ → Name the campaign: [product group]

  7. Use the following settings:
       -   No end date
       -   5 euro daily budget
       -   Manual Targeting
       -   Fixed bids

  8. Create an ad group for a single product (or a few that will use the same keywords, separate products more than with automatic campaigns!) → add the products belonging to this category below 
  9. Choose keyword targeting

  10. Add the search terms you found in the search terms analysis, add them as:

    1.   -   Broad, (This offers the widest traffic exposure. The advertisement may
            appear when a customer searches for your keyword in any order, including
            close variations)
        -   Phrase, (The advertisement may appear when a shopper searches for     
            either the exact phrase of search terms or sequence of words in your search
            terms)
        -   Exact, (In order for the advertisement to show, a shopper’s search term
            must match the search term exactly)

  11. Add negative keywords, some can possibly be found in the client scan. If not, add bleeders (very expensive generic keywords) and related keywords which describe stuff the client does not sell
    *Tip: add different sizes to negative keywords

  12. Quality check: make sure all settings are correct → Click: ‘Submit for review’ in the top right corner

  13. You are now back in Amazon campaign manager, click the campaign you just created and click: 'create ad group'

  14. This opens the same kind of menu as with the automatic campaigns add all the other product groups that fit in this campaign, with the same settings as described above (you have to do this for all campaigns/products/groups)

    * Don't forget: Add all the keywords you are using for this manual campaign to negative keywords in the automatic campaign (also for sponsored ASINs!!!!)

title: Communication via telephone and Google Meet
body:
When you are on the phone or in an online meeting with a client, you are using direct communication. This means that everything you say will be heard by the client directly - this includes mistakes. When using audio and video, be wary of your facial expressions as they play an important role in communicating. 

Be aware of your attitude

Always keep in mind how you come across to the client. You don't want to give them a reason to think badly of you. You want to understand what the client is saying and make sure to speak clearly yourself, so they understand you too.  It is important to appear enthusiastic when speaking to a client. This makes working with you a pleasant experience for the client and allows for communicating to be easier for both parties. Obviously, you do not want to be overly enthusiastic as this might affect your professionalism in the eyes of the client. 

When making a video call, your body language and facial expressions tell the client a lot about you and the way you work. You should always keep an active listening attitude to let the other person know that you are interested and hear what they are saying. Make sure to look at the client during the conversation and don't forget to smile or nod every now and then to confirm that you heard what was said. Always be friendly, even if things are not going the way you or the client wants them to go. In the end, the customer is king.

Talking on the phone

When talking on the phone, you lack visual communication which can be crucial for mutual understanding. Therefore, it is important to make sure that you and the client understand each other. You can do this by saying things like “yes” and “uhuh” to confirm to the client that you are understanding and agreeing to what is said. This is speaking without interrupting them. If you do not understand the client, make sure to ask them nicely to explain what was said.

Note: It is very useful to keep a notebook to write down keywords of what is being said by the client or to write down any occurring questions from your side. 

The conversation

  1. When you ring the phone or answer it,  always start by stating who you are. For example: “Goodmorning, this is [your name] from Brandsom"

  2. Wait for them to answer you and then start the conversation or listen to the client (depending on who is calling). 

  3. You can start the conversation more casually by asking them how their day is going or how they are doing. After that get straight to the point. There is no need for an unnecessarily long conversation since you and the client are working and probably have other things on the agenda as well.

  4. Should you make a mistake or say something wrong: correct yourself right away. 

  5. When ending the phone call, it might be useful to repeat the core of the conversation. If it was about a question: repeat the answer. If it was about something else: summarize it. This way, should you or the client understand something incorrectly, there is still a possibility to ask for more information.

  6. If everything went well, you wish them a nice day and greet the client goodbye.

Talking on Google Meet

This type of conversation is very similar to a phone call. The difference is that you get more assurance whether something is understood correctly. This is thanks to facial expressions and body language that are picked up on camera. You can tell when someone is confused, for example. It is also easier to read the room. However, before you start making jokes, try to figure out what kind of person you're dealing with. Some people you communicate with may not appreciate a joke. 

It is important to close any unnecessary tabs or possible distractions that may interrupt the conversation. This also avoids any unprofessional things being on your screen should you decide to share your screen with the person you're talking to.

Additional tips:

  1. Answer the phone quickly (preferably within three rings)

  2. Always keep a positive attitude

  3. Speak professionally informal (so in Dutch you can use: “je”)

  4. Use the name of the client with moderation throughout the conversation

  5. Keep your focus on the client and remove any possible distractions!

  6. Never talk in a negative way about the company, other clients or your co-workers

  7. Again: take notes during the conversation

  8. Be clear about the goals you and the client have set and when you expect to achieve them 

  9. Ask whether the client has understood you correctly

  10. Always be the last person to hang up the phone


The client decides if they are happy with the outcome

A client’s response indicates whether the communication was successful.  A happy client will thank you for your service and may even compliment you on your communication skills. When a client is unsatisfied with the outcome or does not understand you correctly, do not get angry. Try to stay calm and explain further or repeat yourself until everything is clear for both parties involved. 

Et voilà, you can now communicate business and client-oriented.

title: Different Marketing Goals
body:
Essentially, we distinguish three marketing goals. Brand Protection, Profitable Growth, and Brand Awareness.

Brand Protection can be seen as the 'hold the line' principle. You only use ads to make sure that customers who already know you and want to buy find you. This prevents your customers from being snatched away by a competitor. You focus your advertisements primarily on branded keywords. You also ensure that you keep the advertising spaces on your own product pages occupied. In this way, you try to prevent potential buyers from going to products of other suppliers.

When your Brand Protection campaigns are running well, you will see a high conversion with a low ACOS. Especially branded keywords will have fewer impressions than product-related keywords. However, these will often be cheaper. (E.g. your brand name will have a lower CPC than 'sports shoe')

Profitable Growth is the sweet spot. This is where you ultimately want most of your campaigns to fall. The budget you have is used to generate as much revenue as possible. To achieve this, you will need to constantly fine-tune your campaigns. You will see that, when you fine-tune your campaigns, they will run better and better; the conversion rate will go up and the ACOS will go down. In this framework, you will find a number of campaigns that you can use, but especially within profitable growth, customization is crucial. For example, for electronics of 200 euros per product, you can afford more clicks per order or a higher CPC than for glasses of a few euros. Make sure you always keep the consumer and the product in mind.

Brand Awareness comes into play when the goal is not to make more profit, but to generate awareness. This may be because your brand name is not yet known or because you want to appeal to a new target group. You can also try to grab market share from competitors. These campaigns are generally more expensive because you are competing against the brand protection of other brands. The main KPIs for Brand Awareness is Impressions and NTB orders.

Finally, there are best practice campaigns that apply per platform. An example is the Low Bid campaign on Amazon.

Click on the image to enlarge

title: Sustainability labeling
body:
Bol.com uses the following sustainability labels at the moment:

  • General sustainability: The general sustainability label is about social and/or environment requirements. Such as: water pollution, additives, chemicals and the use of plant protection products. An article must bear one of the approved sustainability labels in order to be labeled as generally sustainable.
  • Energy efficient: The government has made a mandatory energy label for 16 household categories. Depending on the product group, the labels run from F (lowest) to A (highest) or even A +++. The higher the class, the more energy efficient the item is. Energy-efficient products are automatically added to the sustainability categories when the product information has been filled correctly.
  • Fairtrade: Fairtrade stands for fair pricing, decent working conditions, local sustainability and fair trade relations for farmers and workers. Often these labels can be found on clothing, chocolate, coffee, decoration, cosmetics, jewelry and other textiles. An article must be labeled with one of the approved fairtrade trademarks to be labeled fairtrade.  
  • Recycled: Recycled items save valuable resources. Often these products are made from recycled material. On bol.com an item can be labeled ‘recycled’ when the item exists of at least 50% recycled material. Verification of one of the approved labels, or verification provided by the manufacturer is asked to prove the percentage of the recyclable materials. 
  • Refurbished and second-hand: These products are sustainable because they do not use new resources and therefore a more sustainable choice. Refurbished exists of used and repaired components. Second-hand has been used by another used before and gets a new life by reselling. Refurbished and second-hand are not yet identified with a sustainability label on the platform due to technical reasons, but are to be found using the right filters.
  • Organization for a better world: BCorp, Social enterprises and charities. Certificates by BCorp, VSO, ANBI and CBE are needed.
  • Sustainable ingredients: items fit this label when it promotes good cultivation methods, better working conditions and care for the environment. Often these labels can be found on fast consumer goods, cosmetics, food, coffee and tea, and many more product groups. Go to the full list of approved sustainability labels within this theme.
  • Responsible materials: This theme includes products made with independently determined more sustainable materials. These products are produced through responsible forestry, better working conditions and less impact on the environment. Products in this category often fall under fashion, paper or wooden items. Sustainable packaging is not included in this theme. Go to the full list of approved sustainability labels within this theme.
  • Free from harmful substances: Criteria: PFAS free (particularly relevant for pans) or PFC-free (particularly relevant for water-repellent or waterproof clothing)

title: Zalando Attribute Population Tool
body:
Zalando Attribute Tool

Attributes are important to increase the findability and conversion rate of the products that are online on marketplaces. Populating the correct filters ensures your product will also be found once the customer starts filtering in the search results. For example:

  • The customer is looking for a short sleeve dress
  • You onboarded the product, without the "Sleeve Lenght" attribute populated
  • The customer searches for "Dress" and uses the filters to select "Short Sleeve"'
  • Since you didn't fill in the attribute, your product is now excluded from the results

Filling all attributes can be a time-consuming task, therefore we have developed a tool that can populate some of these attributes based on the packshot (usually the main image).

In the template, you can find everything you have to submit, important to keep in mind is:

  • The tool works best with packshots, model shots tend to confuse the tool
  • The category in the template has to be identical to the Zalando category, which can be found in your categorization mapping in your integrator.
  • The tool works best based on ParentSKU, this avoids inconsistencies in the output

Since it is an automated process, you should ALWAYS check the results. You can use the =image formula in Google Sheets to easily place the image next to the results. This should make it very efficient to check the output. 

What attributes can be filled in with the tool, depends on the category your product is in. In the attachments, you will find a filtered list of all attributes that can be populated based on category. Some attributes have a higher success rate than others. 

So far, the tool is only available for Textile products. Accessories and shoes follow later when the tool is optimized. 

If you want to use the tool to populate attributes, send a request to Imco@brandsom.nl with a populated template. The template can be found in the attachments of this course.

NOTE: Do not change the headers in the template, they have to be identical for the tool to work!

title: Sustainability criteria for products
body:
Below you can read more about Zalando’s criteria for products and how Zalando is changing their approach towards upcoming brands according to new compliance with the EU legislation. 

Product Sustainability Q4 2022

The Product Sustainability at Zalando (more info in the partner portal)  has been updated with the new criteria updates that will come into effect as of Q4 2022. We have also outlined the updates to Sustainability Criteria for Products and the updated steps that you must follow to ensure that you fulfil our requirements for product sustainability in the article Changes in our approach to Product Sustainability Q4 2022. 

 

The current criteria and required steps for onboarding new articles (outlined in the article below) will remain valid until the end of September 2022. 

 

By 2023, we want to generate 25% of our GMV with more sustainable products. We have set clear criteria for products to carry our sustainability flag and we continuously raise those standards. We want to highlight all the great work brands are doing in this space and enable customers to make more sustainable decisions.

 

There are different ways for you to tag your products with the Sustainability flag, depending on the material composition or third-party sustainability certifications that we accept. Brand partners are responsible for inputting sustainability information for products hosted on Zalando, and information entered is a legal self-declaration. Please ensure you have all the relevant documentation on hand to prove your product claims, as this is required on request.

 

Within Zalando, we differentiate between external third-party certifications such as the Global Organic Textile Standard, which the brand itself must be certified in order to use, and internal product attributes based on material composition, such as organic or recycled content. Please follow the steps below and ensure that you fulfil our requirements for sustainability claims.

 

Step 1) 

Provide all mandatory information in the article onboarding process. To provide the information correctly you must fill in the details directly in the attributes.

For all labels with minimum material requirements, the sustainable material composition must be specified in the free text field to prove the requirements are fulfilled.


For example: if you have an article consisting of 40% polyester and 60% recycled polyester, the correct way to tag the product would be:

 

1) Select: Made with 50-70% recycled content in the certificate information attribute

2) Map the overall material in the material attribute: 100% Polyester

3) Specify 60% recycled material in the free text or description fields

 

If required, you also may also need to provide:

Certificate Testing Institute / Certification Body

Certificate approval number


Step 2) 

Once all required information is transmitted to Zalando, it is checked against our sustainability guidelines by internal teams.


Step 3) 

Once approved, the Zalando Content Creation team adds the sustainability flag to the relevant products.


On the Zalando platform, all sustainable articles are displayed to customers with Sustainability. It is also possible for the customer to filter directly for sustainable fashion by ticking the Sustainability field.



Sustainability criteria in summary (to be changed in Q4 2022*):

 


https://partnerportal.zalando.com/partners/s/article/Sustainability


More information about Zalando’s current sustainability standards can be found in the partner portal.

title: From/for pricing rule
body:
Introduction:

On May 28, 2022, the new European Consumer Protection Modernization Directive (OMNIBUS) came into force. The new directive includes a provision on the so-called from/for price or as it is called in Dutch ‘Van/voor prijs’.

In brief,

This guideline indicates that for a discount promotion where you want to mention the 'from' price, you must use the lowest sales price that you have conducted within the last 30 days before the price reduction. An example of this includes:

Would you like to mention a starting price or a discount percentage? Then you have to count that from the lowest price you asked in all 30 days before.

 Examples include:

If the product was € 15 for a long time, it may not first be increased to € 25 for a short time and then lowered again to € 15, so that it can be said that there is a 40% discount. After all, the lowest price in those 30 days was already €15, so there is no question of a discount

Suppose on October 1st the price was $100, on October 5th you lowered it to $90, and on October 31st you lowered the price to $89. Then you cannot say that you are giving a €11 discount. After all, the lowest price within 30 days was €90. The discount is therefore only €1,-.

However,

!!!In the Netherlands, it remains permitted to refer to the original price if there is an unbroken succession of price reductions!!! For example:

Please note, this arrangement:

  • Only applies to products, not to (digital) services and digital content;
  • Applies to all distribution channels: physical, online, telephone, catalogue, etc.
  • Concerns notifications from the seller that he has reduced the selling price compared to the previous selling price.

What does this rule not cover?

  • This law does not apply to perishable products
  • This rule does not restrict the entrepreneur's freedom to determine the price himself.
  • The rule does not cover generic communication about all or about categories or ranges of products offered by the seller if no specific measurable price reduction is announced. For example: Lowest price, promotion, sale, outlet, etc.
  • The rule does not cover price comparisons in which the sales price is compared with a recommended sales price, the price charged by a competitor or any other reference price (where it must be clear which type of reference price is used, otherwise it is an unfair commercial practice).
  • The rule does not cover discount cards or vouchers that entitle the consumer to a discount on all or categories or ranges of products offered by the seller.
  • The rule does not apply to offers where the purchase of a product in combination with other products is cheaper. For example, 1+1 free or two for the price of one.
  • The rule does not apply to loyalty programs where consumers receive a discount on future purchases when they purchase a product

This link provides further information about this Law and other general price laws referring to price manipulation:

https://www.acm.nl/nl/onderwerpen/verkoop-aan-consumenten/verkopen-aan-consumenten/prijzen-vermelden/prijzen-vermelden

title: The cost of fast fashion
body:
The fashion industry allocates itself as one of the biggest contributors to our current economy with a value that goes over 2.5 trillion dollars. Its value has increased especially after the beginning of 2000s, with people buying 60% more garments in 2014 and keeping them for less time (McKinsey & Company, 2016).


This incredible growth has brought an impressive negative environmental  impact and the following data speaks for itself: the fashion industry is responsible for 10% of human carbon emissions together with drying up of water resources and river pollution. All of this without taking into account that more than 85% of all textiles go to the dump each year (UNECE, 2018). 


Fast fashion has truly made garments cheap and widely available, skyrocketing clothing production and consumption. This results in most garments becoming easily disposable and replaceable; which brings environmental and social costs, from water pollution caused by untreated dyes and microplastics to poor working conditions and low wages.


Besides the environmental costs the fashion industry presents high rates of underpaid workers and unsafe working conditions. According to Fair Trade Certified, more than 80% garment workers are underpaid women or girls that face a range of health hazards like lung disease due to poor ventilation and synthetic particulates and cotton dust clouds. Also, tasks that require repetitive motion cause musculoskeletal injuries. 


These high environmental and human costs (workers subject to appalling working conditions to produce price competitive items) definitely brings up questions as to how to redesign this industry.

title: Human sustainability
body:
The first pillar is human sustainability. The focus of this pillar is to improve the conditions of human capital. This translates into investments towards health, education, access to services, nutrition and so on.Investments of this type also apply to businesses that see themselves as members of society and therefore considered key players that promote business values that improve human capital. 

In a nutshell:

Human sustainability encompasses the development of skills and human capacity to support the functions and sustainability of the organisation and to promote the wellbeing of communities and society.


From a business perspective this could mean: businesses that actively fund and/or take the time to educate their employees about sustainable practices and that are aware of the impact all their economic driven actions have on human well being.

title: CEO Job Profile
subtitle: The CEO at Brandsom holds the ultimate responsibility for the company’s overall performance and the achievement of all KPIs.
body:
1. The Function

Position Title: CEO
Role Overview:
The CEO at Brandsom holds the ultimate responsibility for the company’s overall performance and the achievement of all KPIs. This includes guiding the strategic direction, upholding the mission, vision, and core values, and ensuring sustainable growth for the organization.

Reporting Line:

  • The CEO reports to the shareholders.
  • The Finance, Product, Sales teams, and the COO report into the CEO.

2. Your Responsibilities

Overall Summary
 The CEO at Brandsom sets the strategic direction, drives sales growth, and ensures the entire organization embodies the company’s core values, mission, and vision.

High-Level Responsibilities

  1. Strategic Leadership
    • Define and communicate the company’s vision, mission, and core values.
    • Develop long-term strategic objectives that position Brandsom for sustainable growth.
  2. Financial Responsibility
    • Own full P&L accountability, ensuring financial health and strategic investments align with business goals.
    • Maintain overhead costs at a target of 30%, and achieve an EBITDA target of 20%.
    • Drive new business/sales growth toward a target of €401k in 2025.
  3. Key Partner Relationships
    • Oversee high-value partnerships with platforms such as bol.com, Zalando, Amazon, ChannelEngine, and Channable.
    • Negotiate and nurture alliances that enhance Brandsom’s market presence and customer value proposition.
  4. KPI Management
    • Ensure the company meets or exceeds overall revenue and sales targets.
    • Monitor overhead costs and EBITDA in line with strategic financial goals.
    • Collaborate with the COO for operational and client-focused KPIs, providing guidance and resources as needed.
  5. Culture & Growth
    • Champion a culture where every team member understands and lives the mission, vision, and core values.
    • Foster a high-performance environment that attracts and retains top talent while encouraging ongoing professional development.

3. How to Make Impact

On the Customer Side

  • Define the strategic direction for sales channels, including social commerce, conversational commerce, and the marketplace roadmap.
  • Collaborate with the Head of Marketplaces & Digital Strategy to align product and service developments with market needs and customer expectations.

On the Process Side

  • Take direct responsibility for the finance and sales processes.
  • Ensure these processes are efficient, transparent, and designed to support the company’s growth objectives.

On the Quality Side

  • Shape the right culture in which people can thrive and succeed, fostering innovation and continuous improvement.
  • Encourage piloting AI and other emerging technologies to stay ahead of industry trends and enhance both internal operations and customer offerings.

4. Your Competencies

Core Knowledge & Skills

  • Proven expertise in start-ups, digital agencies, e-commerce, and marketplace environments.
  • Demonstrated success with performance-based business models, ensuring profitability and sustainable growth.

Leadership & Management Skills

  • Strong leadership capabilities, with a track record of effectively managing diverse teams and guiding them toward shared objectives.
  • Skilled in scaling organizations by setting clear visions, structuring teams, and empowering individuals to excel.

Analytical & Innovation Abilities

  • Visionary approach to AI, actively seeking opportunities to leverage technology for competitive advantage.
  • Solid P&L management experience, with the ability to balance strategic investments against revenue and profitability goals.

5. Your Experience

  • A minimum of 10 years of experience in e-commerce, marketplaces, agencies, or a combination of these.
  • Prior MT (Management Team), C-level, or Director-level experience is essential, showcasing the ability to lead at a high strategic level.

title: Head of Marketplaces & Digital Strategy Job Profile
subtitle: As the leader of innovation and growth, you shape and drive the strategic development of services and solutions across teams and clients.
body:
1. The Function

Job Title: Head of Marketplaces & Digital Strategy

Role Description:

As the leader of innovation and growth, you shape and drive the strategic development of services and solutions across teams and clients. You are responsible for identifying, implementing, and optimizing innovative strategies that enhance team efficiency and deliver measurable results for clients. This includes initiatives such as marketplace growth plans, advanced advertising frameworks, and end-to-end process improvements.

This role focusses on customer and marketplace facing processes and innovations, not on operational processes like planning this part is managed by the COO.

The Head of Marketplaces & Digital Strategy is not operational, this job functions as 'guru' within the organisation.

Key responsibilities include:

  • Development and execution of marketplace growth strategies
  • Enhancement of service delivery through automation and innovation
  • End-to-end optimization of internal customer and marketplace related processes
  • Mentoring multidisciplinary teams
  • Owning the AI strategy within Brandsom, ensuring AI-driven innovations are effectively implemented where applicable

Reporting Line:

The Head of Marketplaces & Digital Strategy reports directly to the CEO and works side-by-side with the Operations Manager to ensure alignment between strategic vision and operational execution.

2. Your Responsibilities

High-Level Responsibilities

  1. Service Development
    • Develop and enhance customer-facing services and tools for marketplaces.
    • Drive the rollout, integration, and adoption of new innovations to improve team efficiency and customer performance.
    • Identify and prioritize key innovations to expand the company’s service portfolio.
    • Oversee AI-driven service enhancements and ensure AI is leveraged effectively where applicable.
  2. Team Development & Support
    • Act as the leading authority on marketplaces, providing strategic guidance to internal teams.
    • Mentor multidisciplinary teams on request (consultants and specialists) to ensure continuous professional growth. Team member annual reviews are managed by the COO, Consultant of team lead.
    • Foster collaboration across departments to enhance teamwork and optimize performance.
    • On request troubleshoot and implement solutions for complex customer challenges.
    • Ensure teams are equipped with AI knowledge and tools to enhance efficiency and decision-making.
  3. Strategic Growth & Process Optimization
    • Align marketplace strategies with corporate vision and objectives.
    • Drive automation and efficiency improvements across service teams.
    • Implement data-driven decision-making to enhance strategic service execution.
    • Leverage AI-driven insights for data analysis, forecasting, and performance optimization.

3. How to Make Impact

On the Customer Side

  • Develop and implement customized marketplace strategies for important long-term client success.
  • Position Brandsom as a thought leader and trusted partner in the marketplace ecosystem.
  • Use AI-driven insights where applicable to enhance personalization and efficiency in client strategies.

On the Process Side

  • Drive automation and efficiency improvements across marketplace services.
  • Implement technology-driven solutions to optimize internal processes.
  • Identify and roll out innovative tools and methodologies to streamline service delivery.
  • Ensure AI is applied where beneficial to automate workflows and improve process intelligence.

On the Quality Side

  • Ensure continuous innovation in service offerings and execution.
  • Develop and integrate cutting-edge marketplace strategies that enhance performance.
  • Maintain a data-driven approach to assess and refine strategic initiatives.
  • Champion AI as a tool for improving analytical capabilities and decision-making processes.

4. Your Competencies

Core Knowledge & Skills

  • 10+ years of experience in marketplaces, with deep expertise in Amazon, Bol.com, Zalando, and other relevant platforms.
  • Strong strategic insight into e-commerce and marketplace developments.
  • Proven ability to drive innovation and implement scalable solutions.
  • Deep understanding of automation, process optimization, and digital transformation.
  • Experience with stakeholder management and cross-functional collaboration.
  • Understanding of AI applications in marketplaces and how to effectively integrate AI-driven solutions.

Leadership & Management Skills

  • Extensive leadership experience, particularly in coaching multidisciplinary teams.
  • Ability to foster buy-in and align teams around strategic objectives.
  • Strong communication and stakeholder engagement skills.
  • Ability to translate AI potential into actionable business applications.

Analytical & Innovation Abilities

  • Highly data-driven, with experience in leveraging analytics to optimize service delivery.
  • Strong problem-solving mindset, with a focus on continuous improvement and automation.
  • Ability to identify and implement cutting-edge innovations, including AI-driven opportunities.

5. Your Experience

  • 10+ years of experience in e-commerce, with a strong focus on marketplace strategy and growth.
  • Broad knowledge of the e-commerce industry and its evolving landscape.
  • Excellent communication skills, both internally and externally.
  • Proven stakeholder management experience, ensuring effective collaboration across teams and partners.
  • Experience in innovation and tool implementation within a consultancy or marketplace environment is a plus.
  • Hands-on experience with AI applications in e-commerce or marketplaces is a plus.

title: Chapter 1: Understanding & Building Custom GPTs
body:
AI Academy: Custom GPTs for Business & Automation

Chapter 1: Understanding & Building Custom GPTs

1. What is a Custom GPT?

A Custom GPT is a version of OpenAI’s ChatGPT that is tailored to specific needs. Instead of using the general ChatGPT model, a Custom GPT can be trained to:
✅ Follow company-specific guidelines
✅ Use specialized terminology
✅ Automate repetitive tasks
✅ Provide answers based on internal knowledge

Custom GPTs are useful when businesses want an AI assistant that understands their brand, industry, and workflow better than a general AI model.

Examples of Custom GPTs

📌 A Customer Support AI that provides accurate, brand-aligned responses to FAQs.
📌 A Consulting AI that answers based on internal playbooks and standard operating procedures (SOPs).
📌 A Zapier Automation Assistant that helps team members build automations without technical knowledge.

By building a Custom GPT, businesses can increase efficiency, consistency, and accuracy in AI-driven interactions.

2. How to Create a Custom GPT (Step-by-Step Guide)

Creating a Custom GPT does not require deep coding knowledge. Here’s a structured guide:

Step 1: Define the Purpose

Ask yourself:
✅ What problem will this Custom GPT solve?
✅ Who will use it (employees, customers, or both)?
✅ What kind of knowledge should it have?

📌 Example: If you're creating a Custom GPT for your consultancy agency, it should specialize in Amazon, Bol, Zalando, AI automation, and performance marketing.

Step 2: Access the OpenAI GPT Builder

  1. Go to OpenAI's Custom GPT page (https://openai.com/gpts)
  2. Click “Create a GPT”
  3. Open the GPT Builder, where you’ll guide the AI step by step.

Step 3: Customize Instructions & Personality

In this step, you’ll define how the GPT should behave.

🔹 System Instructions (How should it respond?)

  • Be formal or casual?
  • Be creative or stick to factual information?
  • Include humor or remain strictly professional?

📌 Example for an Amazon Expert GPT:
"You are a specialized Amazon marketplace expert trained to assist with product listings, SEO optimization, PPC strategies, and sales growth. Keep responses clear, data-driven, and practical. Avoid giving legal or compliance advice."

🔹 Custom Knowledge

  • Upload company documentation, SOPs, product catalogs, FAQs, or previous case studies.
  • This allows the GPT to provide business-specific responses instead of generic ones.

Step 4: Define Capabilities

You can enable:
✅ Web Browsing (optional) – If the GPT needs real-time information.
✅ Code Execution – If it needs to analyze data, generate scripts, or perform calculations.
✅ API Integrations – To connect with internal tools like CRM, Slack, or Zapier.

📌 Example: A Custom GPT for Amazon agencies might have API integrations to pull product sales data from Seller Central.

Step 5: Test & Refine

  1. Ask test questions to see if the AI responds as expected.
  2. Adjust system instructions to fine-tune tone and accuracy.
  3. Collect feedback from test users (employees, clients) and iterate.

Step 6: Deploy & Share

✅ Make the GPT available internally (via Slack, CRM, or a web app).
✅ Train your team on how to use it effectively.
✅ Monitor performance and update it as your business evolves.

3. Use Cases for Custom GPTs

🔹 Internal Use Cases

✅ Knowledge Base AI – Employees can ask SOP-related questions instead of searching through documents.
✅ AI-powered Training Assistant – New hires can get instant answers on company policies.
✅ Automation & Workflow Helper – Guides teams on how to build workflows using Zapier.

🔹 Client-Facing Use Cases

✅ Customer Support Chatbot – Provides instant responses for common inquiries.
✅ Marketplace Seller Assistant – Helps clients optimize Amazon/Bol/Zalando listings.
✅ Lead Qualification AI – Analyzes and ranks inbound leads for your agency.

🔹 Automation & Integration Use Cases

✅ Slack AI Bot – Employees can ask for real-time sales reports or AI-powered insights.
✅ AI-Powered Data Analysis – Summarizes reports and provides insights based on uploaded CSV files.
✅ AI-driven Personalization – Customizes responses based on user input or preferences.

Final Thoughts

Custom GPTs are the next step for businesses looking to leverage AI for specific needs. By following this step-by-step guide, your consultancy agency can build AI tools that provide real value, increase efficiency, and improve decision-making.

title: Chapter 1: ChatGPT-4o – The All-Rounder
body:
What is ChatGPT-4o?

ChatGPT-4o (short for "Omni") is OpenAI’s latest and most advanced model. It is designed to be fast, intelligent, and highly capable of understanding and generating human-like text. It’s great at analyzing data, providing insights, and assisting in creative tasks.

What Type of Requests Work Best?

  • Strategic Thinking & Brainstorming: Need help structuring an Amazon product launch or a marketing campaign? ChatGPT-4o can generate detailed step-by-step plans.
  • Advanced Content Writing: From high-converting Amazon listings to in-depth LinkedIn outreach messages, this model crafts compelling copy.
  • Data Analysis & Insights: You can upload spreadsheets and ask for trends, summaries, and recommendations.
  • Multi-turn Conversations: It keeps track of discussions, making it great for ongoing strategy planning.

Limitations

  • While highly powerful, it does not have real-time internet access (unless connected via plugins or custom integrations).
  • It may not always be the best for executing code-heavy tasks instantly.

title: Introduction
body:
One of the most important parts of working at Brandsom is fulfilling our client's needs. This is simply because they are the reason we get to work. Therefore, you want to build a strong working relationship with the client. To achieve this,  good communication is necessary to figure out their goals and needs and to let them know what we can do to help. After all, you want to keep the client satisfied. How do we do this? And what exactly is good communication and how do we keep it businesslike?

One way of communicating is by e-mail. Another frequently used option is calling the client by phone. It is very important to write and talk in a formal way at the early stages of the business relationship.


When to call, e-mail or plan a Google Meet?

We believe that a periodic meeting structure is the core of good communication with the customer. Besides this, we use the Voys (internet phone) and e-mail.

  • Try to discuss all topics in the (bi)weekly, monthly and quarterly meetings. If you receive e-mails in the meantime discuss the also during these meetings.
  • Second way of communication method is e-mail because it's clearer, faster and more reliable for the customer.
  • Third way of communicating is calling the customer using Voys. Sometimes it's quicker to just call the customer, if you call the customer please use Voys.
  • The customer can call the generic phone number (please check the site for the latest number) and re-routed if needed.


Tone of voice 

When you communicate with a client, it is important to use a formal tone of voice. This indicates the way and the tone in which you write or speak to a client. Your tone of voice should be professional and friendly. This way of communicating will be explained in detail in this module.

title: Communication via E-mail
body:
When writing an e-mail to communicate, you’re using indirect communication. Indirect communication allows you to edit and adjust your message as you go. Here's a useful checklist you can use:

  1. Use the ‘subject’-field

The subject field should contain the core of your message. Do you want to ask a question? Put “Question [topic of question]” in the subject field. Are you responding to a previously sent e-mail? Use “RE: [subject]”. When something is urgent, you put “URGENT” in the subject field. Try to keep the subject to the point.

It’s important to note that the best e-mails use one subject at a time!

  1. Always start with a formal greeting

In English: Dear [Only first name],
In Dutch: Beste [Only first name],

If you do not know the name of the client, which will probably not be the case, you can use the name of the company. Please, do keep it personal.

Using the first and last name of the client is especially important at the beginning of the business relationship. When you are responding to e-mails more frequently, you can start using only the first name of the client. 

  1. Important things first

Put the most important information first. The question(s) you want to be answered, the thing you need from the client etc. This is what the recipient will read first and what will hopefully evoke some kind of curiosity in them to continue reading. Possible explanations or further information follows after. 

Try to avoid starting sentences with “I”, because that can come across as less formal and too demanding. 

  1. Keep your e-mail to the point

Short and to the point e-mails are the way to go. People are more inclined to read your e-mails when they aren't too extensive since a lot of people use their phones to quickly check their inbox. 

  1. Avoid unnecessary information

Try to summarize the core of your message. Again, it is still important to think from the receiver's perspective -  be clear. Also, try to avoid ambiguity. If you feel like something you wrote could cause any sort of trouble or confusion, shortly explain what you mean.

  1. Use paragraphs

If you do need to exchange more information, and writing a short e-mail is not going to get the message across effectively, use paragraphs to organize the information. Paragraphs make long texts easier to read. You can even use headers. 

  1. Avoid abbreviations

Write your words and sentences in full. It is the most formal type of writing. Abbreviations can come across as being rushed.

  1. Ending the e-mail

When you are concluding the email, you can put something you hope for or expect from the recipient in the last sentence. Ending an email with: “I am looking forward to your [response/answer/opinion”, is a good way of showing the receiver that you expect them to respond to your email. 

Use a blank line and always close the email with a greeting. A correct way is by saying: “Kind regards”. In Dutch, one way to conclude your email formally is by saying: “Met vriendelijke groet”. After this, a signature follows.

  1. Your personal signature

It is important to always use your personal signature when writing a business email. Therefore, creating an automatic signature is recommended. We will go more in-depth about how to create your own automatic Brandsom signature later.

  1. Review your e-mail

Read your written e-mail one more time before clicking the send button. This allows you to check for mistakes and gives the opportunity to see if all the information is included and clear for the recipient to understand. Something you write can sound logical to you while it could make no sense to others - again: try to think from the client’s perspective. Also, always check your spelling for any mistakes. Please, do not forget this step!


Conclusion
After a while, you may get a better idea of the relationship with the client. Perhaps clients themselves start using more informal language when communicating with you. Naturally, you may want to go with the flow and do the same. Which is understandable, but never forget that you are still working with them, so try to keep it professional. 

If any mistakes are made on Brandsom’s side, no matter to what extent, the client will always hold you responsible. When using informal language, a client might think you’re not taking things seriously. Which will lead to them being dissatisfied or even worse, end the relationship. To avoid any of these issues, try to use formal language as much as possible.

If you are unsure about setting up e-mails, try to think about the e-mails you receive yourself. Was there ever a time that an e-mail was so unclear that you didn't feel like reading it because it took too much time to understand what was said? Also think about the time you read a very clear e-mail: what did it look like and why was it so clear? 

Still not sure? You can always ask a co-worker for help.

title: Marketplace integrator
subcategory: WIP - Marketplace and E-commerce IT set-up 101
body:
Marketplace integration is the integration of popular, well-established marketplaces that already exist on the internet. It establishes a connection between the marketplace and your online store in order to automate product-related information such as title, description, picture, inventory etc. Examples for marketplaces are large companies with huge inventories like Amazon, Zalando, Rakuten, and bol.com.

A marketplace integrator is an e-commerce tool that serves as a two-way link between multiple online marketplaces and a single selling platform. The purpose of any marketplace is to facilitate transactions between users. To do this, you need to have users who are engaged and committed to your platform. A key aspect of the marketplace model is that anyone (both buyers and sellers) can join the platform for free. The marketplace operator then benefits from all the value creation occurring on the platform.

title: Basic communication model
body:
A basic communication model looks like this:

  • The sender wants the receiver to understand the message exactly as intended

  • This is why the sender tries to convey their message clearly and concisely

  • The interference in communication must therefore be minimized

Common types of interference:

  • Most people that communicate through email expect a response the same day. Try to respond to emails as soon as you can, but do not rush. You still have to respond in a correct and clear way. If you are busy and cannot respond that same day, make sure to let the client know. It is better for clients to know when to expect something than not receiving anything whatsoever.

  • To fully understand the client, always ask what the client means. For example, when a client says something like: “Handle [this] as usual” → What does the client mean by 'as usual? It is important to ask about these types of matters to get confirmation on things. Verifying stuff like this prevents you from wasting extra time and wondering if you are doing things the right way.  Keep in mind to not ask (too many) unnecessary questions, because this makes you come across as not confident in your work skills and abilities.

  • Stay professional and friendly. The type of language you use is very important to keep in mind during the communication process. When your client calls you by your first name, for instance, you do not want to call them by Mr. or Mrs.

  • Always show interest in your client.

How?

  • Know what you want to convey, stay to the point (summarize the core of your message in a single sentence)

  • Transmit the message clearly (phrase your text thinking from the recipient)

  • Speak the recipient's language (avoid jargon and difficult words)

title: Disciplinary Action
body:
Disciplinary Action

At Brandsom, we maintain high standards of conduct and performance. When these standards are not met, we follow a structured disciplinary process to address the issues. The following outlines our disciplinary action procedures

Types of Misconduct:

  1. Minor Misconduct: Includes lateness, unauthorized absence, minor breaches of company policy, and unprofessional behavior.
  2. Serious Misconduct: Includes repeated minor misconduct, insubordination, breach of confidentiality, and misuse of company property.
  3. Gross Misconduct: Includes theft, fraud, physical violence, severe harassment, discrimination, and any criminal activities.

Disciplinary Process:

  1. Informal Warnings: For minor issues, a verbal warning may be given and documented by the supervisor.
  2. Formal Warnings: For more serious issues or repeated minor issues, written warnings will be issued. These may include:
      • First written warning
      • Final written warning
  1. Disciplinary Meetings: A formal meeting between the employee and their supervisor/HR to discuss the misconduct and agree on corrective actions.
  2. Suspension: Temporary suspension with or without pay, depending on the severity of the issue, while an investigation is conducted. This must be done in accordance with Dutch labour laws, ensuring fairness and due process.
  3. Termination: For gross misconduct or failure to improve after previous warnings, termination of employment may be the final step. Termination must follow the procedures outlined in Dutch labour law, including providing notice and the right to appeal.

Employee Rights:

  • Employees have the right to be informed of the allegations against them.
  • Employees have the right to respond to allegations and present their case.
  • Employees have the right to be accompanied by a colleague or representative during formal disciplinary meetings.
  • Employees have the right to appeal any disciplinary decision.

title: The Integrator
body:
The integrator is, as it were, the bridge between the feed and the marketplace. Brandsom works with several integrators such as Tradebyte, ChannelAdvisor, ChannelEngine, Vendiro and ProductFlow. You can see an integrator as a large web in which all information from the feed is linked to the information the marketplace asks for.


Within the integrator, 3 parts are the most important: the product data, the error list and the value mapping. We will briefly explain these 1 by 1.


1 Product data

Almost all information from the feed ends up in the product data overview. Here are the images, the EANs with the corresponding colors and sizes, but also information such as the season and the age recommendation of the product, so the total picture of attributes. If, according to the error list, product information is missing in the integrator, you usually add it here. This is also the place where products are categorized for each marketplace. For example, a shoe at one marketplace could fall under boots, but another marketplace could offer more sophisticated categories and therefore want this product under chelsea boots.


Much of the product information can be divided into two different groups: components and attributes. A component is characterized by the open text fields that are attached to it. Usually this concerns information that is very specific, such as the material composition. You can type this in yourself, or add it via a (bulk) upload.

An attribute offers a (drop down) menu with options that you can choose from, because these are often values that are more general. Think of gender and season. This information can also be added via a (bulk) upload.

2 Value mapping

As soon as the products have been given a category, they become visible in the value mapping. The value mapping is the place in the integrator in which you match the information you have received from the customer with the information that the marketplace asks of you. For example, an asics shoe may have been given the color "hazard orange", and you will therefore have to show the integrator by means of value mapping that this belongs to what Zalando calls "orange". This works the same for other attributes and components: an M is the same as a Medium, Male is the same as Men and SS22 is the same as Spring Summer 2022. Often marketplaces have clearly indicated in the value mapping of their channel which fields are required for their onboarding . They do this by means of an orange triangle in TradeByte, a division in “Mandatory attributes” in ChannelEngine and the standard template in ChannelAdvisor. Because of these different formats you can immediately see which fields you have to fill in on the product data page.


To be able to value mapping, you must first connect the requested information to a “field”. For example, you can connect “Main color” to “color” and then you can map the colors. Sometimes, however, the connections are less obvious. If there is no suitable component or attribute to link to the requested …, we often choose an attribute or component that we know has been completed for each product, such as gender. Suppose you are asked by a fashion customer to map something about the number of batteries supplied, then you may not have a suitable component or attribute for this. If you map it to gender, you can map at once for all men and women that the shoes do not contain batteries.

3 The Error List

When all product data has been added and all values have been mapped, the product should in principle be ready to go live. However, this is not always the case. Sometimes it turns out that something is missing, certain information is incorrect or another problem arises. This can then be seen in the error list of the integrator. You can often make an export of this to create a clear overview of which products are stuck and why. Once these errors have been resolved, it is time for the next phase.

title: Planning responsibilities
body:
Available hours

  • Part-time specialists can register their availability up to six weeks in advance. Two weeks in advance is final, other hours can still be rescheduled.
  • Full-time specialists are scheduled for their hours minus 8 hours. 

Scheduling

  • Planning will be done per team 
  • Consultant is responsible for planning their team including interns en flexers
  • Once per week the planning is finalised in the weekly planning meeting. Consultants and team lead are present at this meeting.
  • HR (Joris) will still be able to help with the availability of Flexers, designers and translators
  • Flexers keep an eye on Everhour to see when they are scheduled and for which customer.

Check

  • The team leads will ensure the process is implemented properly by doing sporadic checks regarding the planned hours.

title: The front end
body:
Congratulations! As soon as you have reached the front-end you are live on the marketplace. However, this does not mean that your integration work is done. Problems can often still arise because a push from the back-end does not run smoothly. For example, it is possible that products with the wrong title are placed online, or within the wrong category. These kinds of problems can be found by means of an on-site check, where you check whether all products are live with the correct information.


Problems can also arise with, for example, the sizes or the images. You often find out by reading the reviews of the product and seeing what customers are complaining about. If you notice this, change it as soon as possible. This way, you can prevent receiving more negative reviews.

title: ChannelEngine’s Customer Support Portal
body:
Here’s how it works:

  1. Go to the ChannelEngine Help Center.
  2. Click the Sign in button at the top right corner.
  3. If you’ve never contacted them before, click Sign up. If you were in touch with them in the past, click Get a password.

You’ll then receive an email prompting you to set up your password, which you can use to log into the portal.

 

What’s new?

Once you’re successfully signed in to your account, a few options become available:

  • Submit a request - at the top of your screen you can find the button Submit a request, which you can use to submit all your support requests from now on. They ask you to provide them with as much detail as possible, which ensures that your request is picked up efficiently by the right person available at the time.
  • My activities - click your name on the top right corner of our Help Center and select My activities. There you can find an overview of all your submitted support requests, as well as their statuses. Click one of your active support requests to reply directly from your newly created account.


For more information on how to best contact the support team, see ChannelEngine: how do I get help?

title: Closing words
body:
Brandsom wants to support growth in its employees. An employee starts as a marketplace specialist. With the right motivation and skills, Brandsom is more than happy to train and educate. You can grow from a marketplace specialist to a junior consultant, and maybe even to a senior consultant in the long run.  

When you first join our team as a marketplace specialist, you will always work under a consultant. Through Asana, they will hand out tasks that need to be completed. In Asana you can log your hours and keep track of your progress on a certain task.

After finishing a task, contact your consultant through either Slack or e-mail and mark your task as "Needs check". When the consultant sees that the task has been finished, they will mark it as complete. 

Finally, to make sure no important information goes missing, everything is uploaded to Google Drive. This way, everyone has access to your work at all times. Try not to save important documents on your computer, but do everything on the drive.

title: Team Asana - Which tasks can you log hours on?
body:
Each team has its own project created in Asana. Here you log hours that do not belong to a particular customer. 

Each team member has some standard task

  • Education task not related to customer
    Only log the hours that are not related to customers. For customer education or onderboarding use the customer task, because these hours are billable.
    IMPORTANT: Only log hours here that are approved by the consultant - related to a specific project/education

  • Brandsom monthly

  • HR
    All your HR-related hours. Think of 1) meetings with HR 2) Performance meetings. Meetings with your manager or consultant that are not related to your personal performance or situation should be logged on the customer.

  • Process improvement
    Brandsom process improvements, improvements that are in favor of the customer should be logged on a project task on customer level. For example: a feed improvement is billable and should be logged on a customer. For example 'Research Collabary framework'.

  • Handover
    Only use for populating hand-over document, other customer hours can be logged on project management tasks on customer level

  • Daily stand-up
  • Team monthly

title: MarktMentor - How to get started
body:
How to get started with MarktMentor?

MarktMentor is a tool developed specifically for the Bol marketplace. The tool offers lots of features, but within Brandsom we use the tool for: 

  • Keyword Research
  • Keyword Tracking on a product level
  • Review Requests

How to add a new client to MarktMentor

  • Click on your Profile at the top right corner and click "Add new store"
  • Fill in the information (name of the store and mail address)

As soon as the store is connected it takes some time to sync all the data. You don't have to configure anything to track keywords, since the tool automatically tracks all products and all keywords.

Setting up Review requests for Bol via MarktMentor

Once the data is synced, you can configure review requests for the client.

  • First, we need to set our VAT settings, you can do this via BTW instellingen in the bottom left corner. All information can be found in the Bol account settings of the client
  • To set reviews, go to "Winkel Beheren" > E-mail campagnes > "Maak Nieuwe Campagne"
  • Set a name for your email campaign, this can be client name
  • Use the settings below:

title: MerchantSpring - Reporting
body:
MerchantSpring - Reporting

One of the most important features within MerchantSpring is the automated reporting that is implemented in the tool. You can report on:

  • A single channel, for example Amazon Germany
  • Multiple Channels, based on a tag.

If you want to split out the reporting for the client, you can do so based on a tag. For example:

  • The client wants to split the reporting between Zalando and Amazon
  • Create a tag with "Client Name - Zalando" and "Client Name - Amazon", this way you can report on all Zalando and Amazon channels in one report. If you only want to report on Zalando DE, you don't have to create a separate tag.
  • You can now create multiple reports, based on the tags you just created
  • You can still send a combined report (if requested by the client) of the complete overview, based on the client tag that is already created. 

What metrics are included in the report?

You will find multiple metrics to report on, where you can find the following main topics:

  • Sales insights - Gives an overview of the total performance of the channels included in the reporting
  • Profitability - Only available for specific channels, and only works if both advertising and COGS are imported
  • Operations - Goes more in-depth on stock levels  
  • Marketing - Shows advertising performance. 

For all clients, the following commercial report should be configured. Based on clients' needs you can extend this report, but at minimum the following should be included, based on "Multi-Channel":

  • Total Business Performance
  • YTD business performance
  • Sales by channel
  • Advertising performance trend
  • Advertising performance by channel
  • Advertising performance summary

If the client wants to receive Profitability reports, create a separate report for the channel you want to report on, only including the profitability metrics.

Use the following settings:

  • Report to be sent once per month (commercial report)
  • Time period = last month
  • Naming convention: Client Name - Monthly Commercial - Report - ADE - PP
    • PP = Prior Period
    • Add all marketplaces that are included, ADE = Amazon DE for example.

For all key marketplaces, set the following operational report. Contains the following metrics. this report is created based on Single Channel:

  • Inventory status
  • Inventory management
  • Top Sellers
  • Worst Sellers
  • Seller Health (extended)
  • Review Status
  • Buybox trend

Use the following settings:

  • Send every week
  • Time period = Current Year
  • Naming convention: Client Name - Weekly Operational - Report - Marketplace

You can consult with the client if the client wants different reporting ranges or revenues excluding taxes. Keep in mind that revenue is after returns.

Also, make sure to save the report and add your client to the mailing list.

Attached:

  • Report showing all metrics that can be included in the report
  • A report showing the mandatory reporting metrics for Monthly Commercial

title: Introduction to Brandsom
body:
Welcome to Brandsom!

We are delighted to have you as a part of our team. At Brandsom, we believe that our success is built on the dedication, skills, and enthusiasm of our employees. This Employee Handbook is designed to provide you with an understanding of our company policies, procedures, and benefits. It serves as a guide to help you navigate your employment experience with us, ensuring that you have all the necessary information to thrive in your role.

The purpose of this Employee Handbook is to:

  • Provide a clear understanding of the company’s expectations and policies.
  • Outline the benefits and resources available to you as an employee.
  • Offer guidance on how to address various work-related situations.
  • Foster a sense of community and collaboration within the company.

In this handbook, you will find detailed information on:

  • Company Policies: Our guidelines on attendance, dress code, confidentiality, and more.
  • Employee Benefits: An overview of the holiday, travel, and financial benefits available to you.
  • Performance and Development: Our approach to performance reviews, professional development, and career growth.
  • Workplace Conduct: Standards for professional behavior and procedures for addressing concerns.
  • Health and Safety: Policies to ensure a safe and healthy work environment.


Your Role and Responsibilities


As a valued member of the Brandsom team, you are expected to adhere to the policies and guidelines outlined in this handbook. Your contributions are vital to our success, and we encourage you to take full advantage of the resources and opportunities available to you.


Open Door Policy

We believe in open communication and encourage you to reach out to your manager, HR representative, or any member of the leadership team with any questions, concerns, or suggestions. Your feedback is essential to our continuous improvement and success.

Conclusion

We are excited to have you join us and look forward to achieving great things together. This handbook is just the beginning of your journey with Brandsom. Welcome aboard!

title: Qualification framework
body:
The exploratory phase is a very important part of any sales strategy; the part where you truly learn how you can best help your prospects, whether they need your help, and whether they want it. It's how you can open your prospects' eyes to a better world - when they'll start to believe that you can help them overcome their challenges and achieve their goals - all within the right timeline and the right budget.


As an inbound salesperson, you'll explore the unique challenges and goals these 'qualified leads' are pursuing to assess whether your solution is the best fit for their context. If it is a good fit, you can convert these leads into opportunities. To do this, you need a qualification framework to work within: CGP, TCI & BA


CGP stands for challenges, goals, and plans. You need a deep understanding of your prospect's challenges, goals, and plans before you can begin to suggest a path forward.


TCI stands for timeline, consequences, and implications. Help your prospect define a timeline for implementing their plans. Get them to articulate the negative consequences of inaction and the positive implications of taking action.


BA stands for budget and authority. Instead of leading with that, save it for the end. Help your prospect understand the vision and what's at stake before you start talking logistics.


Exploring new business opportunities requires you to take in a lot of information. Having a framework helps you accomplish a few really important things:

  • Understanding: You can make sure you don't miss details that are important in understanding your buyer's context.
  • Effective communication: You can have a structure for communicating your prospect's story back to them, helping them know that you heard them.
  • Advising: You can position your products and services as a solution to your buyer's challenges. 

Lastly, this framework will help you in the next part of your inbound sales strategy, the advise phase.
